18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple

42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |

54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from

54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}

57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT

85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special

87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."

100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
160	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
161	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
163	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
164	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
165	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
166	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
167	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
168	  %  the function
169	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
170	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
171	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
172	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
173	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
174	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
175	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
176	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
177	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
178	  \[
179	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
180	  \]
181	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
182	  the tensor product of sections
183	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
184	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
185	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
186	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
187	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
188	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
189	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
190	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
191	  polynomial ring).
192	\end{definition}
193	
194	\begin{definition}
195	[Section graded module]
196	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
198	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
199	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
200	  % Polynomials" (read from
201	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
204	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
205	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
206	  %  the function
207	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
208	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
209	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
210	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
211	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
212	  I.7.}
213	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
214	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
215	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
216	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
217	  \[
218	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
219	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
220	  \]
221	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
222	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
223	  by the section tensor product
224	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
225	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
226	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
227	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
228	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
229	\end{definition}
230	
231	\begin{lemma}
232	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
233	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
234	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
235	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
236	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
237	  % Polynomials" (read from
238	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
239	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
240	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
241	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
242	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
243	  %  the function
244	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
245	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
246	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
247	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
248	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
249	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
250	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
251	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
252	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
253	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
254	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
255	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
256	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
257	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{proof}
261	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
262	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
263	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
264	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
265	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
266	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
267	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
268	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
269	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
270	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
271	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
272	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
273	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
274	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
275	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
276	\end{proof}

278	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
279	  graded series]
280	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
281	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
282	  \mathlibok
283	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
284	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
285	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
286	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
287	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
288	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
289	  equals \(h(n)\):
290	  \[
291	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
292	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
293	  \]
294	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
295	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
296	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
297	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
298	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
299	\end{lemma}
300	
301	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
302	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
304	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule, lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
305	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
306	  % Polynomials" (read from
307	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L459-L464).
308	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
309	  % "$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
310	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
311	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
312	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
313	  %  is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
314	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof)."
315	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Hilbert--Serre,
316	  [Hartshorne], I.7.}
317	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
318	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
319	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
320	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
321	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
322	  such that for all \(n > N\)
323	  \[
324	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
325	  \]
326	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
327	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
328	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\). Applied to \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and
329	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (finitely generated by
330	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), this exhibits the graded Hilbert function
331	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
332	  as the eventual coefficient function of such a rational series.
333	
334	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
335	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
336	  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
337	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
338	  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
339	  rational series from the module.
340	\end{lemma}
341	
342	\begin{proof}
343	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
344	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the ring \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) is Noetherian
345	  and finitely generated over \(\kappa = \kappa(s)\), say by homogeneous
346	  generators \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) of positive degrees, and
347	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
348	  with finite-dimensional components. Passing to a Veronese subalgebra and
349	  re-grading reduces to the case where \(R\) is generated in degree \(1\), so
350	  \(R\) is a graded quotient of a polynomial ring
351	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\). One proves rationality of the Hilbert series by
352	  induction on \(r\): multiplication by \(x_r\) gives a short exact sequence of
353	  graded modules
354	  \(0 \to K \to M(-1) \xrightarrow{x_r} M \to C \to 0\)
355	  with \(K, C\) finitely generated graded modules annihilated by \(x_r\), hence
356	  modules over a polynomial ring in \(r - 1\) variables; additivity of
357	  \(\dim_\kappa\) on the graded components turns the inductive hypothesis for
358	  \(K, C\) into the statement that
359	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is, up to a polynomial, of the
360	  form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\), whence
361	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n = p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) up to a polynomial. The
362	  base case \(r = 0\) has \(M\) finite-dimensional, so \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for
363	  \(n \gg 0\) and the series is a polynomial. Choosing \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\)
364	  (the integer coefficients viewed in \(\mathbb{Q}\)) gives the claim.
365	\end{proof}

367	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
368	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
370	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
371	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
372	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
373	  % Polynomials" (read from
374	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
375	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
376	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
377	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
378	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
379	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
380	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
381	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
382	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
383	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
384	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
385	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
386	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
387	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
388	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
389	  \[
390	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
391	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
392	  \]
393	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
394	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
395	\end{theorem}
396	
397	\begin{proof}
398	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
399	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
400	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
401	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
402	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components. By
403	  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
404	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
405	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
406	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
407	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
408	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
409	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
410	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
411	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
412	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
413	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
414	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
415	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
416	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
417	\end{proof}

419	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
420	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
421	
422	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
423	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
424	geometric conditions that are not yet primitive in the underlying sheaf-of-modules
425	formalism: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support proper over the
426	base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally free of a fixed
427	rank}. This section encodes both predicates. Coherence itself is carried, as in
428	\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule} and the generic-flatness layer, by the
429	two object predicates \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\) and
430	\(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{IsFiniteType}\) already available on a sheaf of modules; the
431	new content here is the support and the rank predicates.
432	
433	\begin{definition}
434	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
435	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
436	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
437	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
438	  % Polynomials" (read from
439	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
440	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
441	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
442	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
443	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
444	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
445	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the construction is a
446	  project-built primitive, see the note below.}
447	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on \(X\) ---
448	  an object of the category \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
449	  which in the formalism is \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\,(X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf})\).
450	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
451	  \[
452	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F}) \in X.\mathrm{IdealSheafData},
453	    \qquad
454	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F}) \;=\;
455	    \mathrm{ofIdeals}\Bigl( U \;\longmapsto\;
456	    \mathrm{Ann}_{\Gamma(X,U)}\,\Gamma(\mathcal{F},U) \Bigr),
457	  \]
458	  which assigns to each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) the annihilator ideal of the
459	  module of sections \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}, U)\) over the ring \(\Gamma(X, U)\).
460	  This is the schematic incarnation of the support of \(\mathcal{F}\): its
461	  vanishing locus is exactly where the stalks of \(\mathcal{F}\) are nonzero.
462	
463	  \emph{Note (project-built primitive).} Mathlib carries no support or annihilator
464	  of a \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\) / \(\mathrm{Scheme.Modules}\); the only support
465	  primitives in algebraic geometry are at the level of ideal sheaves
466	  (\(\mathrm{IdealSheafData.support}\)) and morphism kernels
467	  (\(\mathrm{Hom.ker}\)). The definition above is therefore introduced here,
468	  mirroring \(\mathrm{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.ker}\)
469	  (\texttt{IdealSheaf/Basic.lean}), which is built as
470	  \(\mathrm{ofIdeals}\,(U \mapsto \mathrm{RingHom.ker}\,(f.\mathrm{app}\,U))\) by
471	  the same \(\mathrm{IdealSheafData.ofIdeals}\) constructor
472	  (\texttt{IdealSheaf/Basic.lean}); the affine-local datum is the ring-theoretic
473	  \(\mathrm{Module.annihilator}\) (\texttt{RingTheory/Ideal/Colon.lean}). It is
474	  \emph{not} marked as supplied by Mathlib.
475	\end{definition}

477	\begin{definition}
478	[Schematic support of a sheaf of modules]
479	  \label{def:schematic_support}
480	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupport}
481	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
482	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
483	  % Polynomials" (read from
484	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
485	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
486	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
487	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
488	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
489	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
490	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
491	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
492	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme cut out by
493	  its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
494	  \[
495	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
496	    \;=\; \bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr).\mathrm{subscheme},
497	  \]
498	  equipped with the canonical closed immersion
499	  \(\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr).\mathrm{subscheme}\iota :
500	  \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X\). This uses the
501	  closed-subscheme-of-an-ideal-sheaf machinery
502	  (\(\mathrm{IdealSheafData.subscheme}\) and \(\mathrm{subscheme}\iota\),
503	  \texttt{IdealSheaf/Subscheme.lean}), whose inclusion is automatically a
504	  preimmersion and quasi-compact, hence a closed immersion onto the support of
505	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\). It realises Nitsure's ``schematic support of

509	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
510	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
512	  \mathlibok
513	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
514	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
515	  The class of \emph{proper} morphisms of schemes is the morphism property
516	  \[
517	    \mathrm{IsProper}
518	    \;=\; (\mathrm{IsSeparated} \,\sqcap\, \mathrm{UniversallyClosed})
519	    \,\sqcap\, \mathrm{LocallyOfFiniteType},
520	  \]
521	  i.e.\ a morphism \(f : X \to S\) is proper iff it is separated, universally
522	  closed, and locally of finite type. As a \(\mathrm{MorphismProperty}\) it is
523	  stable under isomorphism, composition, and -- crucially for the Quot functor's
524	  pullback action -- \emph{stable under base change}
525	  (\(\mathrm{IsProper.isStableUnderBaseChange}\)). Consequently the
526	  base-change clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by
527	  base-change'') is discharged for free for the proper-support condition below.
528	\end{lemma}

530	\begin{definition}
531	[Proper support over the base]
532	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
533	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
534	  \uses{def:schematic_support, lem:isProper_mathlib}
535	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
536	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
537	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
538	  % + L418-L419).
539	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
540	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
541	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
542	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
543	  %  [...]
544	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
545	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
546	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
547	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
548	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
549	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite of the canonical closed immersion
550	  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) with \(f\) is proper:
551	  \[
552	    \mathrm{HasProperSupport}\,f\,\mathcal{F}
553	    \;:\Longleftrightarrow\;
554	    \mathrm{IsProper}\Bigl(
555	      \bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr).\mathrm{subscheme}\iota \;\circ\; f
556	    \Bigr),
557	  \]
558	  where \(\mathrm{IsProper}\) is the Mathlib morphism property of
559	  \cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}. Reusing \(\mathrm{IsProper}\) rather than
560	  re-bundling separatedness, universal closedness, and finite type by hand makes
561	  the base-change stability of proper support immediate, which is precisely what
562	  the well-definedness of the Quot functor's pullback action requires.
563	\end{definition}
564	
565	\begin{definition}
566	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
567	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
568	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
569	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises (2)" and
570	  % "Grassmannian of a vector bundle" (read from
571	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L562-L564
572	  % + L584-L585).
573	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
574	  % "$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
575	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
576	  %  [...]
577	  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients
578	  %  $q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,"
579	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
580	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules and let \(r \in \mathbb{N}\). We say
581	  \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank \(r\)} when there is a witness
582	  exhibiting \(\mathcal{M}\), locally on an open cover, as the free sheaf of
583	  modules on the finite index set \(\{1, \dots, r\}\):
584	  \[
585	    \mathrm{IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\,\mathcal{M}\,r
586	    \;:\Longleftrightarrow\;
587	    \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(\text{local-trivialisation data of }\mathcal{M}
588	      \text{ against } \mathrm{free}(\mathrm{Fin}\,r)\bigr),
589	  \]
590	  the data consisting of an open cover of the base together with, on each member,
591	  an isomorphism of the restriction of \(\mathcal{M}\) with
592	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.free}\,(\mathrm{Fin}\,r)\) (the coproduct of \(r\)
593	  copies of the unit module sheaf).
594	
595	  \emph{Encoding note.} The rank is carried as the parameter \(r\) of the local
596	  model \(\mathrm{free}(\mathrm{Fin}\,r)\); it is \emph{not} a rank-agnostic
597	  local-freeness predicate conjoined with a sheaf-level
598	  \(\mathrm{rankAtStalk} = r\) condition, because no sheaf-level
599	  \(\mathrm{rankAtStalk}\) exists in Mathlib (it is a ring-level invariant
600	  \(\mathrm{Module.rankAtStalk}\) on \(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum}\)). The shape
601	  parallels the existing object predicates on a sheaf of modules ---
602	  \(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,\mathcal{M} := \mathrm{Nonempty}\,
603	  (\mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\,\mathcal{M})\) and
604	  \(\mathrm{IsFinitePresentation}\) (\texttt{Sheaf/Quasicoherent.lean}),
605	  \(\mathrm{IsFiniteType}\) (\texttt{Sheaf/Generators.lean}) --- and is built on
606	  the free-sheaf machinery \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.free}\),
607	  \(\mathrm{freeFunctor}\), and \(\mathrm{mapFree}\)
608	  (\texttt{Sheaf/Free.lean}). If the trivialisation isomorphisms are needed as
609	  data downstream, the predicate splits into a data carrier and its
610	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\) propositional truncation, exactly as
611	  \(\mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\) / \(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\) do. It is
612	  \emph{not} marked as supplied by Mathlib.
613	\end{definition}

618	\begin{definition}\leanok
619	[Quot functor]
620	  \label{def:quot_functor}
621	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
622	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
623	
624	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
625	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
626	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
627	  % + L481-L498).
628	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
629	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
630	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
631	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
632	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
633	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
634	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
635	  %
636	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
637	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
638	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
639	  %
640	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
641	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under

643	  %
644	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
645	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
646	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
647	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
648	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
649	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
650	  %  well-defined, which gives
651	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
652	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
653	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
654	  %  [...]
655	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
656	  %  as a co-product
657	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
658	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
659	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
660	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
661	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
662	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
663	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
664	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
665	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
666	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
667	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
668	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
669	  \[
670	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
671	  \]
672	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
673	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
674	  \begin{enumerate}
675	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
676	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) and which is flat over \(T\).
677	      Coherence is encoded by the two object predicates
678	      \(\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\) and
679	      \(\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsFiniteType}\) (as in
680	      \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}); the properness of the schematic
681	      support over \(T\) is \(\mathrm{HasProperSupport}\) of \(\mathcal{F}\)
682	      along the structure morphism \(X_T \to T\)
683	      (\cref{def:has_proper_support});
684	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
685	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
686	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
687	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
688	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
689	      \(\Phi\).
690	  \end{enumerate}
691	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
692	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
693	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
694	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
695	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
696	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).

699	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
700	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
701	
702	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
703	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
704	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
705	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
706	
707	\begin{definition}\leanok
708	[Grassmannian scheme]
709	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
710	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
711	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
712	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
713	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
714	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
715	  % + L773-L850).
716	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
717	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
718	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
719	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
720	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
721	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
722	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
723	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
724	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
725	  %  contravariant functor
726	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
727	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
728	  %  set of all equivalence classes
729	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
730	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
731	  %  [...]
732	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
733	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
734	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
735	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
736	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
737	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
738	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
739	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
740	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
741	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
742	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
743	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
744	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
745	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
746	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
747	  \[
748	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
749	    \qquad
750	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
751	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
752	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
753	  \]
754	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of the
755	  quotient \(\mathcal{F}\) is the predicate
756	  \(\mathrm{IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\,\mathcal{F}\,d\)
757	  (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}). Concretely \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
758	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) (the Quot functor for \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
759	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).
760	
761	  Structurally \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\) is \emph{defined} as this Quot functor
762	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) of the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
763	  \(\Phi = d\) -- not as a second, independently-built functor. Fixing the
764	  definition this way makes the identity
765	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) hold by
766	  construction, so the structural correspondence is preserved automatically
767	  when the underlying \(\Quot\) construction is later filled in, and the
768	  Grassmannian inherits its functoriality and equivalence relation from the
769	  Quot functor rather than restating them.
770	\end{definition}

862	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
863	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
864	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
865	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
866	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
867	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
868	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
869	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
870	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
871	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
872	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
873	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
874	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
875	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
876	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
877	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
878	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
879	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
880	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
881	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
882	
883	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
884	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
885	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
886	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
887	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
888	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
889	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
890	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
891	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
892	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
893	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
894	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
895	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
896	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
897	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
898	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
899	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
900	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
901	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
902	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
903	\end{proof}

\bigskip

\centerline{\large\bf The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and $\Quot_{E/X/S}$}

\medskip

The above functors $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ and $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$
admit the following simple generalisations. 
Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme and let $X\to S$ be a finite type
scheme over it. Let $E$ be a coherent sheaf on $X$. 
Let $Sch_S$ denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
over $S$. For any $T\to S$ in $Sch_S$, a \textbf{family of quotients of $E$ 
parametrised by $T$} will mean 
a pair $(\F,q)$ consisting of 

(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$ 
such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$ 
and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with 

(ii) a surjective ${\mathcal O}_{X_T}$-linear homomorphism of sheaves 
$q:E_T  \to \F$ where $E_T$ is the pull-back of $E$ under
the projection $X_T \to X$.

Two such families $(\F,q)$ and $(\F,q)$ parametrised by $T$ will 
be regarded as equivalent if $\ker(q) = \ker(q')$), 
and $\langle \F, q \rangle$ will denote

\textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the construction is a
  project-built primitive, see the note below.}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on \(X\) ---
  an object of the category \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
  which in the formalism is \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\,(X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf})\).
  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
  \[
    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F}) \in X.\mathrm{IdealSheafData},
    \qquad
    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F}) \;=\;
    \mathrm{ofIdeals}\Bigl( U \;\longmapsto\;
    \mathrm{Ann}_{\Gamma(X,U)}\,\Gamma(\mathcal{F},U) \Bigr),
  \]
  which assigns to each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) the annihilator ideal of the
  module of sections \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}, U)\) over the ring \(\Gamma(X, U)\).
  This is the schematic incarnation of the support of \(\mathcal{F}\): its
  vanishing locus is exactly where the stalks of \(\mathcal{F}\) are nonzero.

  \emph{Note (project-built primitive).} Mathlib carries no support or annihilator
  of a \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\) / \(\mathrm{Scheme.Modules}\); the only support
  primitives in algebraic geometry are at the level of ideal sheaves
  (\(\mathrm{IdealSheafData.support}\)) and morphism kernels
  (\(\mathrm{Hom.ker}\)). The definition above is therefore introduced here,
  mirroring \(\mathrm{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.ker}\)
  (\texttt{IdealSheaf/Basic.lean}), which is built as
  \(\mathrm{ofIdeals}\,(U \mapsto \mathrm{RingHom.ker}\,(f.\mathrm{app}\,U))\) by
  the same \(\mathrm{IdealSheafData.ofIdeals}\) constructor
  (\texttt{IdealSheaf/Basic.lean}); the affine-local datum is the ring-theoretic
  \(\mathrm{Module.annihilator}\) (\texttt{RingTheory/Ideal/Colon.lean}). It is
  \emph{not} marked as supplied by Mathlib.

The class of \emph{proper} morphisms of schemes is the morphism property
  \[
    \mathrm{IsProper}
    \;=\; (\mathrm{IsSeparated} \,\sqcap\, \mathrm{UniversallyClosed})
    \,\sqcap\, \mathrm{LocallyOfFiniteType},
  \]
  i.e.\ a morphism \(f : X \to S\) is proper iff it is separated, universally
  closed, and locally of finite type. As a \(\mathrm{MorphismProperty}\) it is
  stable under isomorphism, composition, and -- crucially for the Quot functor's
  pullback action -- \emph{stable under base change}
  (\(\mathrm{IsProper.isStableUnderBaseChange}\)). Consequently the
  base-change clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by
  base-change'') is discharged for free for the proper-support condition below.
input.new_string:
A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.

\textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite of the canonical closed immersion
  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) with \(f\) is proper:
  \[
    \mathrm{HasProperSupport}\,f\,\mathcal{F}
    \;:\Longleftrightarrow\;
    \mathrm{IsProper}\Bigl(
      \bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr).\mathrm{subscheme}\iota \;\circ\; f
    \Bigr),
  \]
  where \(\mathrm{IsProper}\) is the Mathlib morphism property of
  \cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}. Reusing \(\mathrm{IsProper}\) rather than
  re-bundling separatedness, universal closedness, and finite type by hand makes
  the base-change stability of proper support immediate, which is precisely what
  the well-definedness of the Quot functor's pullback action requires.

\textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules and let \(r \in \mathbb{N}\). We say
  \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank \(r\)} when there is a witness
  exhibiting \(\mathcal{M}\), locally on an open cover, as the free sheaf of
  modules on the finite index set \(\{1, \dots, r\}\):
  \[
    \mathrm{IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\,\mathcal{M}\,r
    \;:\Longleftrightarrow\;
    \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(\text{local-trivialisation data of }\mathcal{M}
      \text{ against } \mathrm{free}(\mathrm{Fin}\,r)\bigr),
  \]
  the data consisting of an open cover of the base together with, on each member,
  an isomorphism of the restriction of \(\mathcal{M}\) with
  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.free}\,(\mathrm{Fin}\,r)\) (the coproduct of \(r\)
  copies of the unit module sheaf).

  \emph{Encoding note.} The rank is carried as the parameter \(r\) of the local
  model \(\mathrm{free}(\mathrm{Fin}\,r)\); it is \emph{not} a rank-agnostic
  local-freeness predicate conjoined with a sheaf-level
  \(\mathrm{rankAtStalk} = r\) condition, because no sheaf-level
  \(\mathrm{rankAtStalk}\) exists in Mathlib (it is a ring-level invariant
  \(\mathrm{Module.rankAtStalk}\) on \(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum}\)). The shape
  parallels the existing object predicates on a sheaf of modules ---
  \(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,\mathcal{M} := \mathrm{Nonempty}\,
  (\mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\,\mathcal{M})\) and
  \(\mathrm{IsFinitePresentation}\) (\texttt{Sheaf/Quasicoherent.lean}),
  \(\mathrm{IsFiniteType}\) (\texttt{Sheaf/Generators.lean}) --- and is built on
  the free-sheaf machinery \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.free}\),
  \(\mathrm{freeFunctor}\), and \(\mathrm{mapFree}\)
  (\texttt{Sheaf/Free.lean}). If the trivialisation isomorphisms are needed as
  data downstream, the predicate splits into a data carrier and its
  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\) propositional truncation, exactly as
  \(\mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\) / \(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\) do. It is
  \emph{not} marked as supplied by Mathlib.

\textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
  \(d\)} when there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) together with
  isomorphisms \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus d}\) for
  each \(i\).
